5AA00Ma07TI.title: Learn Chichewa Language For Tourists and beginners
5AA00Ma07TI.prompt_used: This is a Chichewa (Nyanja) lesson for tourists and beginners. The instructor teaches basic Chichewa greetings and phrases. Common Chichewa: moni (hello), muli bwanji (how are you), ndili bwino (I am ...

segments.text: Hello! Welcome to Say It in Chichewa. Are you a tourist interested in learning Chichewa
language or a Malawian native that want to learn some basic English? Here are 20 essential
keywords and phrases you need to know. Let's begin!
How are you? Muli bwanji. We are or I am fine and you?
Ndili bwino kayainu. What is your name?
Dina lako ndani. I am Samantha.
What about you? Nanga iwe. Where are you coming from?
Machokela kuti. I am coming from Sweden.
Where are you going? Mukupita kuti. I am going to
Dikupita ku. For example, Machitsanzo. I am going to the lake.
Dikupita kunyanja. I am going to the market.
Dikupita kunsika. I am going home.
Dikupita kunyumba. How much?
Zingati. Do you have?
Mulindi. For example, Machitsanzo. Do you have water?
Mulindi mazi. Do you have food?
Mulindi chakuja. Do you have beer?
Mulindi moa. Do you have drinks?
Mulindi zakumwa. Do you have a room?
Mulindi chipinda. Where is the toilet?
Chimbudzi chidikuti. I am sorry.
Pepani. There isn't.
Palibe. I don't have.
Dilibe. No.
Ayi. Yes.
Eya. Thank you.
Zikomo. I appreciate.
Tatokoza. You are welcome.
Takulandilani. See you.
Tionana.

5AA00Ma07TI.full_text: Hello! Welcome to Say It in Chichewa. Are you a tourist interested in learning Chichewa language or a Malawian native that want to learn some basic English? Here are 20 essential keywords and phrases you need to know. Let's begin! How are you? Muli bwanji. We are or I am fine and you? Ndili bwino kayainu. What is your name? Dina lako ndani. I am Samantha. What about you? Nanga iwe. Where are you coming from? Machokela kuti. I am coming from Sweden. Where are you going? Mukupita kuti. I am going to Dikupita ku. For example, Machitsanzo. I am going to the lake. Dikupita kunyanja. I am going to the market. Dikupita kunsika. I am going home. Dikupita kunyumba. How much? Zingati. Do you have? Mulindi. For example, Machitsanzo. Do you have water? Mulindi mazi. Do you have food? Mulindi chakuja. Do you have beer? Mulindi moa. Do you have drinks? Mulindi zakumwa. Do you have a room? Mulindi chipinda. Where is the toilet? Chimbudzi chidikuti. I am sorry. Pepani. There isn't. Palibe. I don't have. Dilibe. No. Ayi. Yes. Eya. Thank you. Zikomo. I appreciate. Tatokoza. You are welcome. Takulandilani. See you. Tionana.

vIwvBKHqD4g.title: Learn Chichewa Language For Tourists and beginners 2
vIwvBKHqD4g.prompt_used: This is a Chichewa (Nyanja) lesson for tourists and beginners. The instructor teaches basic Chichewa greetings and phrases. Common Chichewa: moni (hello), muli bwanji (how are you), ndili bwino (I am ...

segments.text: Hi, welcome back to Say It in Chichewa. Are you new in Malawi and want to engage in a
casual conversation? Then this video is for you. Here are the three most common greetings
used in an informal conversation in Malawi. Bo or bobo. A majority of Malawians, especially the
youth, peers, and children, use the word bo or bobo to say hi. The response is simply bo or bobo.
This phrase you will hear everywhere in the streets of Malawi. Don't be afraid to try it
yourself. The locals will be very happy to see a tourist say bo or bobo. Zikuteka.
The word zikuteka is used informally to say is everything okay? The response is simply also
zikuteka, everything is fine. Oeya zikuteka, yes, everything is fine. Oayisi zikuteka,
no, everything is not fine. Use this phrase for younger adults or the youth to impress.
Zikuyenda. This is used interchangeably with zikuteka, so don't get surprised if you are
asked either or. Zikuyenda literally means is everything going on all right? The response
is also simply repeating the same word zikuyenda, everything is all right. Or add the word ayi
meaning no and add a prefix si to say si zikuyenda meaning it's not going on all right.
Example dialogue. Bo. Bobo. Zikuteka. Zikuteka. Or. Bobo. Bo. Zikuyenda.
Ayi. Zikuyenda.
If your response is negative, be ready to receive a follow-up question such as
What is it?
meaning what is the problem?
Then you can state what the problem is starting with the same statement
Vuto ndi. The problem is for example
I am broke
I am sick
Vuto ndi kutopa. I am tired
Vuto ndi matsire. Vuto ndi matsire. I am hungover
Thank you for watching this video. If you want to learn more Chichewa language and Malawian culture
please like comment and subscribe to our channel Zikomo. Thank you

vIwvBKHqD4g.full_text: Hi, welcome back to Say It in Chichewa. Are you new in Malawi and want to engage in a casual conversation? Then this video is for you. Here are the three most common greetings used in an informal conversation in Malawi. Bo or bobo. A majority of Malawians, especially the youth, peers, and children, use the word bo or bobo to say hi. The response is simply bo or bobo. This phrase you will hear everywhere in the streets of Malawi. Don't be afraid to try it yourself. The locals will be very happy to see a tourist say bo or bobo. Zikuteka. The word zikuteka is used informally to say is everything okay? The response is simply also zikuteka, everything is fine. Oeya zikuteka, yes, everything is fine. Oayisi zikuteka, no, everything is not fine. Use this phrase for younger adults or the youth to impress. Zikuyenda. This is used interchangeably with zikuteka, so don't get surprised if you are asked either or. Zikuyenda literally means is everything going on all right? The response is also simply repeating the same word zikuyenda, everything is all right. Or add the word ayi meaning no and add a prefix si to say si zikuyenda meaning it's not going on all right. Example dialogue. Bo. Bobo. Zikuteka. Zikuteka. Or. Bobo. Bo. Zikuyenda. Ayi. Zikuyenda. If your response is negative, be ready to receive a follow-up question such as What is it? meaning what is the problem? Then you can state what the problem is starting with the same statement Vuto ndi. The problem is for example I am broke I am sick Vuto ndi kutopa. I am tired Vuto ndi matsire. Vuto ndi matsire. I am hungover Thank you for watching this video. If you want to learn more Chichewa language and Malawian culture please like comment and subscribe to our channel Zikomo. Thank you
